description: Figure 1 presents integrated thermodynamic equilibrium calculations for Fe–10Al–15Mn–0.8C–5Ni wt% low-density steel. Panel (a) is a pseudo-binary phase diagram showing liquid (L), δ-ferrite, austenite (γ), and κ-carbide phase fields, with marked peritectic and eutectoid reactions. Panel (b) plots equilibrium phase fractions versus temperature, capturing the progressive reduction of δ-ferrite, the disorder-to-order transition from δ to B2 intermetallic at 1125 °C, and the eutectoid reaction at 851 °C involving γ decomposition. Panels (c) and (d) report equilibrium solute distributions in the γ-matrix and B2-phase, respectively. Near the 900 °C annealing temperature, the γ-matrix retains high Mn (~17 wt%) and moderate Al (~8 wt%), while the B2-phase becomes strongly enriched in Al and Ni with negligible carbon solubility. Collectively, these diagrams establish that solute partitioning from γ into B2 stabilizes Fe(Ni,Mn)Al-type B2 stringers, providing the thermodynamic rationale for controlling B2 volume fraction and morphology during hot rolling and annealing to obtain the desired ultrahigh strength and ductility.

This figure presents the microstructural evolution of Fe–10Al–15Mn–0.8C–5Ni wt% low-density steel across processing stages. Panel (a) is an optical micrograph of the as-cast sample, showing δ-ferrite dendrites (labeled δ, dark) in an austenite (γ) matrix (light), with the caption reporting approximately 27% δ-ferrite. Panel (b) shows the hot-rolled (HR) condition, where δ-ferrite transforms into lamellar B2 stringer bands aligned along the rolling direction (RD), spanning multiple deformed austenite grains. Panel (c) displays the cold-rolled (CR) condition, revealing further thinning of B2 stringer bands and a pancaked γ morphology. Panel (d) is an FESEM image of the HR sample, revealing intragranular B2 (IG-B2) precipitation nucleated at shear bands within the γ matrix. Collectively, the images demonstrate that thermomechanical processing converts cast δ-ferrite into B2 intermetallic stringers and triggers IG-B2 precipitation, establishing the critical microstructural architecture for optimizing strength and ductility.

FESEM micrographs displaying the microstructural evolution of Fe–10Al–15Mn–0.8C–5Ni wt% low-density steel annealed at 900 °C for 5 min (A5; a,d,g), 15 min (A15; b,e,h), and 30 min (A30; c,f,i). Lower-magnification views (a–c) reveal retained B2 stringer bands and coarse unrecrystallized austenite (URE-γ) grains maintaining a pancake structure along the rolling direction. Higher-magnification images (d–i) resolve two distinct B2 precipitate populations: intragranular IG-B2 nucleated within URE-γ shear bands, and coarse intergranular GB-B2 decorating austenite boundaries. IG-B2 is finest in A5 and progressively coarsens into needle-shaped particles through coalescence in shear bands by A30, whereas GB-B2 remains comparatively coarse across all annealing times due to enhanced boundary solute flux. The figure establishes that annealing time strongly modulates the size, morphology, and distribution of B2 precipitates while B2 stringer bands and URE-γ pancake grains persist, underscoring the microstructural basis for tailoring precipitation strengthening and ductility in this lightweight steel.

Figure 4 comprises a bright-field TEM micrograph (a) and TEM-EDX elemental distribution maps (b–f) of the standard-processed A30 sample (annealed at 900 °C for 30 min) of Fe–10Al–15Mn–0.8C–5Ni wt% low-density steel. Panel (a) reveals irregular GB-B2 precipitates along prior austenite grain boundaries, needle-shaped IG-B2 inside unrecrystallized austenite, and sporadic annealing twins; the inset highlights heterogeneous B2 nucleation on annealing twin boundaries. Panels (b)–(f) show Fe, Mn, C, Al, and Ni maps, respectively, indicating that the B2 phase is enriched in Fe, Al, and Ni, depleted in C, and annotated as Mn-depleted in the precipitate. The negligible carbon signal in B2 confirms its identity as an intermetallic rather than a carbide, supporting the reported Fe(Ni,Mn)Al chemistry. Together, these images demonstrate that B2 precipitation—occurring at grain boundaries, twin boundaries, and intragranular sites—partitions substitutional solutes away from the austenite matrix, rendering the B2 phase nonshearable and enabling precipitation strengthening via Ashby–Orowan dislocation looping, which is central to the ultrahigh strength and crack resistance of the steel.

Figure 5 presents the standard-processed Fe–10Al–15Mn–0.8C–5Ni low-density steel. Panel (a) shows engineering stress–strain curves for hot-rolled (HR), cold-rolled (CR), and 900 °C annealed samples (A5, A10, A15, A30), with an inset listing toughness values in kJ mol⁻¹ (J cm⁻³): HR 0.48 (66.5), CR 0.11 (15.2), A5 0.64 (90.1), A10 0.69 (95.8), A15 0.85 (117.8), and A30 0.89 (123.3). CR reaches ~1500 MPa but shows negligible ductility, whereas HR exhibits only ~6.8 % elongation; annealing raises ductility non-monotonically, with A15 tensile strength higher than A10 and A30 ductility increasing only slightly despite longer annealing. Panels (b)–(e) are optical and SEM micrographs of deformed tensile gauges: (b) A5 shows B2 stringer decohesion; (c) A10 reveals micro-cracks along thick B2 stringers perpendicular to loading; (d) A15 displays void nucleation inside B2 stringers; and (e) A30 exhibits micro-crack coalescence with B2 stringer segment debonding. Panel (f) is an EBSD orientation map of A30, revealing grain boundaries within the B2 stringer bands, indicating a bamboo-like statically recrystallized structure. Collectively, the figure demonstrates that thick B2 stringer bands undergo grain-boundary decohesion and microvoid coalescence during tensile deformation, triggering biaxial failure and explaining the unsatisfactory strength–ductility balance in the standard processing route.

Figure 6 presents the mechanical response and deformed microstructure of modified-process Fe–10Al–15Mn–0.8C–5Ni wt% low-density steel. Panel (a) displays engineering stress–strain curves for the hot-rolled (HR), cold-rolled (CR), and annealed samples (A5, A15, A30). The modified thermomechanical processing—high-speed hot rolling at 10 rpm, cold rolling with a 1 mm thickness reduction, and annealing at 900 °C—produces a yield strength near 1 GPa. A5 exhibits the highest strength, while longer-annealed A30 achieves the best ductility and a peak toughness of 1.72 kJ mol⁻¹ (238.38 J cm⁻³), as listed in the inset. Panel (b) shows Hollomon plots (lnσ versus lnε) revealing two-stage strain hardening: an initial low-exponent stage (n ≈ 0.054–0.069) followed by a steady rise (n = 0.179 for A5 to 0.27 for A30) as annealing time increases. Panels (c–e) are optical micrographs of the deformed tensile gauge sections for A5, A15, and A30, respectively, showing B2 stringer bands and micro-cracks. These images demonstrate reduced crack susceptibility in the modified microstructure, supporting the conclusion that refined B2 stringers and a softer γ-austenite matrix delay crack propagation and stringer decohesion, enabling an ultrahigh strength–ductility–toughness combination.

Figure 7 presents multimodal microstructural characterization of Fe–10Al–15Mn–0.8C–5Ni wt% low-density steel, comparing cold-rolled (CR) and interrupted tensile-deformed (e = 0.05) conditions in sample A15. Panels (a) and (b) display an EBSD phase map and corresponding Kernel Average Misorientation (KAM) map for the CR state, revealing co-deformation of austenite (γ) and B2 stringer bands with preferential strain localization in the softer γ matrix, indicated by higher KAM values (scale 0–5). Panel (c) is a TEM bright-field image after interrupted tensile testing at plastic strain e = 0.05, directly showing deformed γ and B2 phases with higher dislocation density in γ. Panels (d) and (e) provide post-deformation EBSD phase and KAM maps at the same strain, identifying B2 particles, deformed γ, γ-boundaries, and deformed B2 stringers. The KAM map in (e) explicitly marks geometrically necessary dislocation (GND) localization at γ/B2 interfaces and near grain boundaries. Together, the images demonstrate strain partitioning between soft γ and hard B2 intermetallic, where dislocations released from γ localize at nonshearable GB-B2 and IG-B2 phase boundaries, reducing bulk GND density and limiting the overall strain-hardening exponent to 0.19–0.27.
